History
[
edit
]
19th century
[
edit
]
Paul Reuter
, the founder of Reuters (photographed by
Nadar
,
c.
1865
)
Paul Julius Reuter worked at a book-publishing firm in
Berlin
and was involved in distributing radical pamphlets at the beginning of the
revolutions of 1848
. These publications brought much attention to Reuter, who in 1850 developed a prototype news service in
Aachen
using
homing pigeons
and
electric telegraphy
from 1851 on, in order to transmit messages between
Brussels
and Aachen,
[
10
]
in what today is Aachen's Reuters House.
Reuter moved to
London
in 1851 and established a
news wire agency
at the
London Royal Exchange
. Headquartered in London, Reuter's company initially covered commercial news, serving banks, brokerage houses, and business firms.
[
11
]
The first newspaper client to subscribe was the London
Morning Advertiser
in 1858, and more began to subscribe soon after.
[
11
]
[
12
]
According to the
Encyclopædia Britannica
: "the value of Reuters to newspapers lay not only in the financial news it provided but in its ability to be the first to report on stories of international importance."
[
11
]
It was the first to report
Abraham Lincoln
's assassination in
Europe
, for instance, in 1865.
[
11
]
[
13
]
In 1865, Reuter incorporated his private business, under the name Reuter's Telegram Company Limited; Reuter was appointed managing director of the company.
[
14
]
In 1870 the press agencies French
Havas
(founded in 1835), British Reuter's (founded in 1851) and German Wolff (founded in 1849) signed an agreement (known as the Ring Combination) that set 'reserved territories' for the three agencies. Each agency made its own separate contracts with national agencies or other subscribers within its territory. In practice, Reuters, who came up with the idea, tended to dominate the Ring Combination. Its influence was greatest because its reserved territories were larger or of greater news importance than most others. It also had more staff and
stringers
throughout the world and thus contributed more original news to the pool. British control of cable lines made London itself an unrivalled centre for world news, further enhanced by Britain's wide-ranging commercial, financial and imperial activities.

details.intent: After the visit node returns structured article records, synthesize a concise comparative summary (approx. 3–6 short bullets + 1 short paragraph) highlighting similarities and divergences in framing, policy focus, geographic/regulatory emphasis, stakeholders cited (governments, companies, researchers), and tone (caution vs innovation). Provide direct references to each article by headline and URL.
details.inputs_expected: Array of extracted article records from the visit node (each with headline, publication_date, main_point, source_name, url, quote).
synthesis_plan: Group articles by policy focus (e.g., EU regulation, US legislative activity, industry self-regulation, enforcement actions).
Identify common themes (e.g., calls for standards, safety, transparency) and differences (e.g., speed of regulation vs innovation priorities).
Note any unique facts or claims and whether they appear corroborated by other articles.
Produce a short comparative summary and a 3–5 bullet list of key contrasts and commonalities, each bullet referencing the article(s) that support it.
details.why_this_step_reduces_uncertainty: Synthesizing ensures we don't just present isolated article facts but derive a clearer picture of the policy conversation, making differences in perspective explicit and supported by citations.
verification_steps: Ensure each claim in the summary cites at least one extracted article by headline + URL.
Flag any assertions not directly supported by extracted facts for follow-up searches/visits.

details.link_idea: Recent news article about artificial intelligence regulation from a major news outlet (Reuters, NYTimes, FT, Guardian, Bloomberg, Politico, BBC, CNN, Axios)
details.intent: Visit two distinct news-website articles (different domains) discovered by the preceding search and extract the required structured facts for each: (1) headline, (2) publication date, (3) main topic/key point (one-sentence summary), (4) source website name. Also capture the article URL and one representative quote/sentence supporting the identified main point.
extraction_plan: Use URLs from the search node; if a direct URL is provided by the executor, visit that exact URL. Otherwise, use vector-store to select top 2 matching links per link_idea from the search output.

For each visited page, extract headline (H1 or meta:title), the byline and publication date, first or summary paragraphs to ascertain main topic/key point, and the site name (from page metadata or domain).
Record extracted fields in structured JSON for each article and include the full visited page text (up to visit limit) and any inline links encountered.
verification_steps: Confirm each article's domain is different from the other to satisfy 'at least 2 different news websites.'
Confirm publication date is within the past 12 months; if not, mark and skip to next candidate link.
Cross-check headline/date with search snippet; if mismatch, prefer page-extracted values and record discrepancy.
details.why_this_step_reduces_uncertainty: Direct page visits provide authoritative, citable fields (headline, date, main point). Visiting two different domains ensures we capture multiple perspectives rather than a single outlet's framing.
measurable_output_fields: articles: [ {headline, publication_date, main_point, source_name, domain, url, quote, author} ]
details.notes: If the first 2 links chosen are from the same domain, the visit action will select the next-best link from the search results (or vector DB) to ensure domain diversity. All visited links will be saved into link collections for future semantic retrieval.

550998c6-561e-4d29-b2c7-b7aae191326f.title: Think: Synthesize and compare perspectives from extracted articles (post-visit analysis)

details.intent: After the visit node returns structured article records, synthesize a concise comparative summary (approx. 3–6 short bullets + 1 short paragraph) highlighting similarities and divergences in framing, policy focus, geographic/regulatory emphasis, stakeholders cited (governments, companies, researchers), and tone (caution vs innovation). Provide direct references to each article by headline and URL.
details.inputs_expected: Array of extracted article records from the visit node (each with headline, publication_date, main_point, source_name, url, quote).
synthesis_plan: Group articles by policy focus (e.g., EU regulation, US legislative activity, industry self-regulation, enforcement actions).
Identify common themes (e.g., calls for standards, safety, transparency) and differences (e.g., speed of regulation vs innovation priorities).
Note any unique facts or claims and whether they appear corroborated by other articles.
Produce a short comparative summary and a 3–5 bullet list of key contrasts and commonalities, each bullet referencing the article(s) that support it.
details.why_this_step_reduces_uncertainty: Synthesizing ensures we don't just present isolated article facts but derive a clearer picture of the policy conversation, making differences in perspective explicit and supported by citations.
verification_steps: Ensure each claim in the summary cites at least one extracted article by headline + URL.
Flag any assertions not directly supported by extracted facts for follow-up searches/visits.
details.goal: Think: Synthesize and compare perspectives from extracted articles (post-visit analysis)
details.original_goal: Think: Synthesize and compare perspectives from extracted articles (post-visit analysis)
details.parent_goal: Search for recent news articles about 'artificial intelligence regulation' from the past year. Visit at least 2 different news websites (not Wikipedia) and extract: (1) The headline of each article, (2) The publication date, (3) The main topic or key point, (4) The source website name. Provide a summary comparing the different perspectives.

d21112b9-deec-4ba1-a0a2-ee410d60403f.title: Save: Persist extracted article facts and summary to memory/namespace

details.intent: Store the structured article records and the comparative summary so they can be referenced later and for reproducibility.
data_to_save: articles (as extracted from Visit node): headline, publication_date, main_point, source_name, domain, url, quote, author
meta: search query and search result snippet list
details.why_this_step_reduces_uncertainty: Persisting results prevents re-running identical searches and preserves the exact evidence used in the summary; it supports verification and future follow-ups.
verification_steps: Confirm all saved article records include a URL and publication date; otherwise mark the record incomplete and do not save until complete.
Return saved record IDs and a checksum of saved content.
